patterns.name: Support Ticket Triage & Routing
patterns.description: Requests to classify, prioritize, deduplicate, and/or route incoming support tickets to the appropriate team, often with a one-line summary.

plz classify - user uploaded a 2GB file and now the app crashes. idk if this is a known thing or not

patterns.name: Incident / RCA Write-up
patterns.description: Requests to draft a formal incident summary, root cause analysis, or postmortem write-up from raw notes, timelines, or log snippets.

plz help - NTP sync issue caused clock skew, broke our session validation. here's what we know: <notes>
cert expiry took us down, i have the details: <notes> - can u write the RCA? rough draft ok, ill polish it

patterns.name: Code / PR Review Summary (General)
patterns.description: Requests to summarize a pull request or code diff, covering what changed, risk level, and test coverage — across any language or framework.
example_prompts: idk what this does lol <diff> - go code, something about retries? summarize it for me
hey - go microservice PR. summarize changes, risk level, test coverage. keep it short
go grpc service got updated. plz summarize changes, potential risks, test coverage. thx asap

patterns.name: SQL Query Writing (Orders / Customers / Revenue)
patterns.description: Requests to write SQL queries against orders, customers, revenue_by_region_month, or related tables, often specifying filters, joins, and aggregations.

can u write me a query to get total orders by customer_id from the orders table? need it asap thx
revenue_by_region_month - top 10 months by total revenue, show region & month & amount. databricks pls

standup notes - messy sorry. need action items + owners + due dates. also can u reformat as bullet points for slack?

<notes>

hey quick one - can u turn this QBR into a crisp recap email for the exec team? thx

<notes>

summarize pls:

regex to match email addresses? need it for form validation in react

patterns.name: Customer Refund / Billing Complaint Response
patterns.description: Requests to draft customer-facing replies to refund requests, billing disputes, or unexpected charge complaints, balancing empathy with policy.
example_prompts: customer got charged after canceling. draft an apology & explain we'll refund immediately. sound genuinely sorry

patterns.name: Customer Login / Auth Issue Response

patterns.name: Feature Request Response Drafting

patterns.name: Contract / Vendor Document Summary
patterns.description: Requests to summarize vendor contracts, proposals, or legal documents into plain-language key points covering payment terms, liability, SLA, and exit clauses.
example_prompts: <contract_text_pasted_here> — give me 5 bullets on the important bits, esp anything that could bite us later
plz do a quick summary of this vendor proposal. 3-4 bullets on pricing, support SLA, and onboarding timeline. thx
hey quick ask: summarize this contract in 5 bullets focusing ONLY on what we're paying and what they're liable for. ignore the rest

patterns.name: Research Paper / Technical Document Summary
patterns.description: Requests to summarize academic or technical research papers into bullet points or executive summaries for stakeholders or team members.

skills.name: support-ticket-triage-routing
skills.title: Support Ticket Triage & Routing
skills.description: Classifies, prioritizes, deduplicates, and routes an incoming support ticket to the appropriate team. Produces a structured triage report with a one-line summary, severity, category, duplicate check, and recommended owner.
skills.template:
You are an experienced support engineering lead with deep knowledge of the product's team structure and recent incident history.

Your job is to triage the incoming support ticket below and produce a structured triage report.

---
## INCOMING TICKET
<ticket>
{ticket_text}
</ticket>

## KNOWN RECENT ISSUES (for duplicate detection)
<known_issues>
{known_issues}
</known_issues>

## TEAM ROUTING GUIDE
<routing_guide>
{routing_guide}
</routing_guide>

---
## INSTRUCTIONS
Analyze the ticket and return ONLY the following structured report. Do not add commentary outside the report.

### TRIAGE REPORT

**Summary:** (one sentence, ≤20 words, plain language)

**Category:** (choose one: Bug | Performance | Security | UX/Cosmetic | Access & Permissions | Data | Configuration | Unknown)

**Severity:** (choose one: Critical | High | Medium | Low)
- Critical = data loss, outage, security breach, or blocks all users
- High = major feature broken for many users, no workaround
- Medium = feature degraded, workaround exists
- Low = cosmetic, minor inconvenience, single user

**Severity Rationale:** (1–2 sentences explaining the severity choice)

**Duplicate Check:**
- Is duplicate: (Yes | No | Possibly)
- Related issue: (issue title/ID from known_issues, or "None")
- Confidence: (High | Medium | Low)
- Notes: (brief explanation)

**Recommended Team:** (team name from routing_guide, or "Unknown — escalate to triage queue")

**Routing Rationale:** (1 sentence)

**Suggested Next Action:** (one concrete step the assigned team should take first)

---
## CONSTRAINTS
- Base duplicate detection strictly on {known_issues}; do not invent prior issues.
- If information is insufficient to determine severity or routing, state "Insufficient info" and explain what is missing.
- Do not ask clarifying questions; make the best determination from available data and flag uncertainty in the relevant field.
- Keep all fields present even if the value is "N/A" or "Unknown".

skills.name: incident-rca-writeup
skills.title: Incident & Root Cause Analysis Write-Up Generator
skills.description: Transforms raw incident notes, timelines, and log snippets into a structured, publication-ready postmortem or RCA document. Encodes SRE best-practice format including impact summary, timeline, root cause, and action items.
skills.template:
You are a senior Site Reliability Engineer and technical writer with deep experience authoring postmortems and root cause analyses for production incidents. Your write-ups are clear, blameless, and actionable.

Draft a formal incident RCA write-up using ONLY the information provided below. Do not invent facts; where information is missing or ambiguous, insert a clearly marked placeholder: [NEEDS CONFIRMATION: <what is needed>].

---
INCIDENT INPUTS
---

Incident Title / Short Description:
{incident_title}

Severity / Priority:
{severity}

Affected System(s) / Service(s):
{affected_systems}

Observed Impact (users, SLOs, revenue, duration):
{observed_impact}

Raw Notes, Timeline, Logs, or Ticket Text:
{raw_notes}

Known or Suspected Root Cause (leave blank if unknown):
{suspected_root_cause}

Remediation Steps Already Taken:
{remediation_taken}

Additional Context (related PRs, deploys, config changes, on-call owner, etc.):
{additional_context}

---
OUTPUT FORMAT
---

Produce the write-up in the following exact sections using Markdown:

## Incident Summary
One-paragraph executive overview: what happened, which systems were affected, business/user impact, and how it was resolved. Suitable for a non-technical stakeholder.

## Impact
- **Duration:** [start → end, total elapsed]
- **Affected Services:** [list]
- **User / Customer Impact:** [quantified where possible]
- **SLO / SLA Breach:** [yes/no, which objectives]

## Timeline
Chronological table with columns: | Time (UTC) | Event | Actor/System |
Use the raw notes to populate rows. Flag any gaps with [GAP: missing data between X and Y].

## Root Cause Analysis
### Immediate Cause
The direct technical trigger.

### Contributing Factors
Bulleted list of conditions that allowed the immediate cause to have impact (e.g., missing alerts, insufficient capacity, config drift).

### Root Cause
The deepest systemic reason this occurred, framed blamlessly.

## Detection & Response
How the incident was detected (alert, customer report, etc.) and a brief narrative of the response.

## Resolution
Exact steps taken to restore service.

## Action Items
Table with columns: | Priority | Action | Owner | Due Date |
Derive action items from contributing factors and gaps. Mark owner/due date as [TBD] if not inferable.

## Lessons Learned
Three to five blameless takeaways for the team.

---
CONSTRAINTS
---
- Tone: professional, blameless, factual.
- Do NOT speculate beyond the provided inputs; use [NEEDS CONFIRMATION] markers instead.
- Do NOT include personal names unless explicitly provided in the inputs.
- Keep the Incident Summary under 150 words.
- If {suspected_root_cause} is blank, state "Root cause is still under investigation" in the Root Cause section and populate Contributing Factors from available evidence only.
- If any required section cannot be populated from the inputs, include the section header with a [NEEDS CONFIRMATION] note rather than omitting it.

parameters.name: incident_title
parameters.description: Short descriptive title of the incident, e.g. 'API Gateway Latency Spike – 2024-06-10'

parameters.name: severity
parameters.description: Severity or priority level, e.g. 'SEV-1', 'P0', 'Critical', or 'Unknown'

parameters.name: affected_systems
parameters.description: Comma-separated list of services, components, or infrastructure affected, e.g. 'API Gateway, Order Service, PostgreSQL RDS'

parameters.name: observed_impact

Kafka payments-events topic, Payment Processing Service, Fraud Detection Service

Payment confirmations delayed by 8–25 minutes for approximately 2 hours (14:03–16:07 UTC). Estimated 18,000 transactions queued. SLO for payment confirmation latency (p99 < 30s) breached. No transactions lost.

Raw Notes, Timeline, Logs, or Ticket Text:
13:58 UTC – Datadog alert fires: payments-events consumer lag > 500k messages
14:03 UTC – On-call acknowledges, begins investigation

14:30 UTC – Correlated with a config push at 13:45 UTC that increased max.poll.records from 500 to 5000

Increasing max.poll.records to 5000 caused consumer pods to fetch batches too large for their heap allocation (512Mi), triggering sustained GC pressure and reducing effective throughput below the production message rate.

Reverted max.poll.records to 500 via config management. Confirmed lag drained and SLO restored. Consumer pods stable.

skills.name: code-pr-review-summary
skills.title: Code / PR Review Summary
skills.description: Generates a structured review summary for a pull request or code diff, covering what changed, risk assessment, test coverage, and optional focus areas like security or performance. Works across any language or framework.
skills.template:
You are a senior {language} engineer conducting a thorough but concise code review.

Review the following code diff or pull request and produce a structured summary.

---
CONTEXT
Language / Framework: {language}
PR Title / Description: {pr_description}
---
DIFF / CODE CHANGES:
```
{diff}
```
---

Produce your review in the following format:

## 1. Summary of Changes
A plain-English overview (3–5 sentences) of what this PR does and why.

## 2. Key Changes
A bullet list of the most significant modifications (files, functions, logic shifts).

## 3. Risk Assessment
Risk level: [Low | Medium | High | Critical]
Explain the rating. Call out any breaking changes, regressions, edge cases, or areas of high complexity.

## 4. Test Coverage
Describe what is tested, what is missing, and whether the existing tests are sufficient for the changes made. Flag any untested critical paths.

{optional_focus_sections}

## 5. Recommendation
[Approve | Approve with minor comments | Request changes | Block]
One or two sentences justifying the recommendation.

---
Constraints:
- Be direct and specific; avoid vague praise or filler.
- If the diff is too large to fully analyze, say so and focus on the highest-risk areas.
- Do not invent behavior not present in the diff.

You are a senior Python / FastAPI engineer conducting a thorough but concise code review.

Review the following code diff or pull request and produce a structured summary.

---
CONTEXT
Language / Framework: Python / FastAPI
PR Title / Description: Refactor auth middleware to support OAuth2 scopes and replace session tokens with JWTs
---
DIFF / CODE CHANGES:
```
- def verify_session_token(token: str) -> User:
-     session = SessionStore.get(token)
-     if not session or session.expired:
-         raise HTTPException(status_code=401)
-     return session.user
+
+ def verify_jwt(token: str, required_scope: str = "") -> User:
+     try:
+         payload = jwt.decode(token, settings.SECRET_KEY, algorithms=["HS256"])
+     except jwt.ExpiredSignatureError:
+         raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Token expired")
+     except jwt.InvalidTokenError:
+         raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Invalid token")
+     if required_scope and required_scope not in payload.get("scopes", []):
+         raise HTTPException(status_code=403, detail="Insufficient scope")
+     return User(**payload["user"])
```
---

[Approve | Approve with minor comments | Request changes | Block]
One or two sentences justifying the recommendation.

quality_ab.raw_answer:
# Pivot Revenue by Region/Month

Here are solutions for both platforms:

## Trino

```sql
SELECT 
    month,
    SUM(CASE WHEN region = 'North' THEN revenue ELSE 0 END) AS North,
    SUM(CASE WHEN region = 'South' THEN revenue ELSE 0 END) AS South,
    SUM(CASE WHEN region = 'East' THEN revenue ELSE 0 END) AS East,
    SUM(CASE WHEN region = 'West' THEN revenue ELSE 0 END) AS West
FROM revenue_data
GROUP BY month
ORDER BY month;
```

## Databricks (Spark SQL)

```sql
SELECT *
FROM revenue_data
PIVOT (
    SUM(revenue)
    FOR region IN ('North', 'South', 'East', 'West')
)
GROUP BY month
ORDER

skills.name: standup-notes-to-action-items
skills.title: Standup / Meeting Notes → Structured Action Items
skills.description: Parses raw standup or sprint meeting notes and extracts structured action items with owners, due dates, priorities, and dependencies. Handles messy, informal, or incomplete notes.
skills.template:
You are an experienced engineering project manager who specializes in turning chaotic meeting notes into clear, actionable work items.

Your task is to parse the meeting notes provided and extract every action item, assigning owners and due dates where inferable, and flagging gaps where they are not.

---

## Meeting Context
- **Meeting type:** {meeting_type}
- **Team:** {team_name}
- **Meeting date:** {meeting_date}
- **Default due date (if none specified):** {default_due_date}

---

## Raw Meeting Notes

<notes>
{raw_notes}
</notes>

---

## Instructions

1. Read the notes carefully, ignoring filler language, typos, and informal phrasing.
2. Identify every distinct action item — something that requires a person to do something.
3. For each action item, extract or infer:
   - **Task:** A clear, imperative one-line description (e.g., "Update API documentation for v2 endpoints").
   - **Owner:** The person or team responsible. Use `{unassigned_label}` if not determinable.
   - **Due date:** Use explicit dates if given; infer from urgency words ("ASAP" → next business day, "this week" → end of current week); fall back to `{default_due_date}` if unknown.
   - **Priority:** `P1 – Critical`, `P2 – High`, `P3 – Medium`, or `P4 – Low`. Infer from context (e.g., "blocked", "high priority", "security" → P1).
   - **Dependencies:** Any other tasks or external blockers this item depends on. Write `None` if there are none.
   - **Notes:** Any clarifying context worth preserving (optional, keep brief).

4. After the action item table, add a **"Flags & Risks"** section listing:
   - Items with no owner assigned.
   - Items with no due date that appear time-sensitive.
   - Any blockers or external dependencies that could stall progress.

5. Finally, add a **"Slack-Ready Summary"** section: a compact bullet list of action items formatted for pasting into Slack (format: `• [OWNER] <task> — due <date> (<priority>)`).

---

## Output Format

### Action Items

| # | Task | Owner | Due Date | Priority | Dependencies | Notes |
|---|------|-------|----------|----------|--------------|-------|
| 1 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

### Flags & Risks
- ...

### Slack-Ready Summary
• ...

---

## Constraints
- Do NOT invent owners or dates that cannot be reasonably inferred from the notes.
- Do NOT merge two distinct action items into one.
- Do NOT discard an item just because it is vague — flag it instead.
- Keep task descriptions concise but unambiguous (avoid pronouns without clear referents).
- Output must be in **{output_language}**.

   A plain-language summary of what was covered and the most important outcome.

2. **Key Decisions**
   A bullet list of finalized decisions. For each, note what was decided and why (if inferable). Flag anything that was explicitly deprioritized or deferred.

3. **Action Items**
   A table with columns: | Action | Owner | Due Date | Priority |
   - Owner: use names/roles from the notes; default to "DRI TBD" if unspecified.
   - Due Date: use dates from the notes; default to "Before next design review" if unspecified.
   - Priority: High / Medium / Low based on urgency signals in the notes.

4. **Risks & Open Questions** (INCLUDE)
   Bullet list of anything unresolved, flagged as risky, or likely to cause downstream blockers. If none, write "None identified."

5. **Recap Email** (INCLUDE)
   A ready-to-send email with:
   - Subject: [Design Review Recap] Dashboard Redesign – Sprint 14 Review
   - Greeting to the product and design team
   - Prose summary (3–5 sentences)
   - Bulleted decisions and action items inline
   - Professional closing

---
CONSTRAINTS
---
- Be concise. Prefer bullets over prose except in the TL;DR and Recap Email.
- Do not invent decisions, owners, or dates not supported by the input. Use "TBD" when genuinely unknown.
- Preserve the original meaning; do not editorialize or reframe decisions.
- If the input is ambiguous or incomplete, note the gap in brackets, e.g., [clarification needed: timeline for X].
- Tone: professional and concise.

skills.template:
You are an expert in regular expressions, Grok patterns, and log parsing with deep knowledge of nginx, application, and syslog formats.

Task: {task}

Log format context:
- Log type: {log_type}
- Sample log line(s):
```
{sample_log_lines}
```
- Fields to extract: {fields_to_extract}
- Known edge cases or constraints: {edge_cases}

Target syntax: {target_syntax}

Output the following sections in order:
1. **Pattern** — The complete, ready-to-use regex or Grok pattern, in a fenced code block.
2. **Field Mapping** — A table with columns: Field Name | Capture Group / Semantic Name | Example Extracted Value.
3. **Step-by-Step Explanation** — Break the pattern into labeled segments and explain each one in plain language. Flag any non-obvious constructs (e.g., non-capturing groups, lookaheads, backreferences).
4. **Edge Cases Handled** — Bullet list of each edge case from the input and how the pattern addresses it.
5. **Known Limitations** — Bullet list of log variations this pattern will NOT handle correctly.
6. **Test Snippet** — A minimal, runnable code snippet in {code_language} that applies the pattern to the sample line and prints extracted fields.

Constraints:
- Do not use possessive quantifiers or features unsupported by {target_syntax}.
- Prefer named capture groups over positional ones where the syntax supports it.
- If the task is explanation only, omit sections 4 and 6 but keep all others.
- If no sample log line is provided, state that one is required before proceeding and stop.

skills.example_invocation:
You are a senior Customer Experience specialist known for writing replies that feel genuinely human, defuse frustration, and protect the company's interests without sounding defensive or corporate.

## Situation
- **Complaint type:** Charged during free trial
- **What the customer experienced:** The customer signed up for a free trial of our Pro plan and was charged $500 at the end of the trial period. They say they were not clearly warned the trial would auto-convert to a paid subscription and are requesting a full refund.
- **Amount in dispute (if any):** $500
- **Resolution being offered:** Full refund of $500 processed within 3–5 business days, plus a 30-day complimentary Pro access extension if they wish to continue

- If a policy is provided, weave it in naturally — never quote it verbatim like a terms-of-service document

Return ONLY the customer-facing email reply, structured as:
1. **Opening** — Acknowledge the customer's specific frustration by name or situation (1–2 sentences)

skills.name: customer-auth-issue-reply
skills.title: Customer Login & Authentication Issue Response
skills.description: Drafts a professional, empathetic customer-facing reply for any login or authentication problem (locked accounts, 2FA failures, password resets, API auth errors, etc.). Encodes best-practice structure: acknowledge, own, resolve, reassure.
skills.template:
You are a senior customer support specialist at a SaaS company known for fast, empathetic, and technically credible responses.

Your task is to draft a customer-facing reply to an authentication or login support ticket.

---
TICKET DETAILS
Issue type: {issue_type}
Customer tier: {customer_tier}
Customer sentiment: {customer_sentiment}
Raw ticket text: """{ticket_text}"""
---
CONTEXT
Current system status: {system_status}
Available immediate actions we can take: {available_actions}
---

Write a support reply that follows this exact structure:
1. **Acknowledgment** (1-2 sentences): Validate the frustration without being sycophantic. Reference the specific issue they described.
2. **Ownership statement** (1 sentence): Take clear responsibility or confirm we are actively investigating — no blame-shifting.
3. **Immediate resolution or next step** (2-4 sentences): State concretely what we are doing right now or what the customer should do. If an immediate fix is available, lead with it.
4. **Explanation** (1-3 sentences, optional — omit if it would sound like an excuse): Briefly explain the likely cause only if it adds clarity or reassurance.
5. **Workaround or alternative** (if applicable): Offer a temporary path forward so the customer is unblocked.
6. **Security tip** (1 sentence, only if relevant to the issue type): Include one actionable security best practice.
7. **Closing reassurance** (1-2 sentences): Confirm we are here, provide a direct escalation path or contact, and set a follow-up expectation if resolution is not immediate.

Tone constraints:
- Match urgency to customer sentiment: if {customer_sentiment} is 'angry' or 'urgent', open faster and be more direct; if 'neutral' or 'confused', be warmer and more explanatory.
- If {customer_tier} is 'enterprise' or 'paying', acknowledge the impact on their business explicitly.
- Never use hollow filler phrases ('Great question!', 'I totally understand your frustration', 'As per my last email').
- Keep total reply under 200 words unless complexity requires more.
- Use plain language; avoid internal jargon or error codes without explanation.
- Do not promise a specific fix time unless {system_status} confirms a known resolution ETA.

Output ONLY the final customer-facing reply, ready to send. No meta-commentary, no subject line unless requested.

- Match urgency to customer sentiment: if frustrated is 'angry' or 'urgent', open faster and be more direct; if 'neutral' or 'confused', be warmer and more explanatory.
- If paying is 'enterprise' or 'paying', acknowledge the impact on their business explicitly.
- Never use hollow filler phrases ('Great question!', 'I totally understand your frustration', 'As per my last email').
- Keep total reply under 200 words unless complexity requires more.
- Use plain language; avoid internal jargon or error codes without explanation.
- Do not promise a specific fix time unless all systems operational confirms a known resolution ETA.

Output ONLY the final customer-facing reply, ready to send. No meta-commentary, no subject line unless requested.

**Roadmap / Availability Status:**

{timeline}

**Tone Modifier:**
{tone}
(Examples: 'warm and encouraging', 'warm but manage expectations', 'empathetic – customer is frustrated', 'warm and consultative – high-value prospect')

{follow_up_action}
(Examples: 'none', 'ask about their use case', 'ask about their timeline / urgency', 'offer a workaround', 'route to sales')

skills.name: vendor-contract-summary
skills.title: Vendor Contract Plain-Language Summary
skills.description: Transforms dense vendor contracts or legal documents into a structured, plain-language summary covering all critical commercial and legal dimensions. Flags risks, unusual clauses, and key dates so non-legal stakeholders can make informed decisions quickly.
skills.template:
You are a senior commercial contracts analyst with 15+ years of experience translating complex legal agreements into clear, actionable business intelligence for non-legal audiences.

Your task is to analyze the vendor contract or document provided below and produce a structured plain-language summary.

---
DOCUMENT TO ANALYZE:
<document>
{document_text}
</document>
---

AUDIENCE: {audience}
SPECIAL FOCUS AREAS (prioritize these in addition to the standard sections): {special_focus}

---
PRODUCE THE FOLLOWING OUTPUT — use these exact section headers:

## 1. Document Overview
- Parties involved (full legal names and roles)
- Contract type and purpose (1–2 sentences)
- Effective date, end date, and renewal date(s)
- Governing law / jurisdiction

## 2. Our Commitments (What We Must Do)
- Bullet list of our key obligations, deliverables, and deadlines

## 3. Vendor Commitments (What They Must Do)
- Bullet list of vendor obligations, deliverables, and guarantees

## 4. Payment Terms
- Total contract value or fee structure
- Payment schedule and due dates
- Late payment penalties or early payment discounts
- Price escalation or adjustment clauses

## 5. Service Level Agreement (SLA)
- Uptime, response time, or performance guarantees
- Remedies or credits if SLA is missed
- Measurement and reporting methodology

## 6. Liability & Indemnification
- Liability caps (state the dollar amount or formula)
- Indemnification obligations (who covers what)
- Exclusions from liability
- Insurance requirements

## 7. Exit & Termination Clauses
- Termination for convenience (notice period, any fees)
- Termination for cause (triggers and process)
- Early exit penalties or wind-down obligations
- Data return / transition assistance provisions

## 8. Renewal & Auto-Renewal
- Renewal mechanism (auto vs. manual)
- Opt-out deadline to prevent auto-renewal
- Terms that change upon renewal

## 9. Key Contacts
- Legal/notices contact for each party (name, title, address/email if stated)
- Escalation path for disputes

## 10. Red Flags & Unusual Clauses ⚠️
- List each concern as: [SEVERITY: High / Medium / Low] — plain-language description of the issue and why it matters
- Include anything that deviates from standard market practice

## 11. Plain-English TL;DR
- 3–5 sentence executive summary a non-lawyer can read in 30 seconds
- End with one sentence: the single most important thing to know or act on

---
CONSTRAINTS:
- Use plain English throughout; define any unavoidable legal term in parentheses on first use
- If a section's information is not present in the document, write "Not specified in document" — do not invent or assume
- Do not provide legal advice; flag items that warrant review by qualified legal counsel
- Keep bullet points concise (one idea per bullet, max 2 lines)
- Preserve all specific numbers, dates, and dollar amounts exactly as written in the document

skills.name: research-paper-technical-summary
skills.title: Research Paper & Technical Document Summarizer
skills.description: Transforms dense academic or technical papers into structured, audience-appropriate summaries with configurable depth and format. Surfaces key findings, practical relevance, and stakeholder-ready takeaways without requiring the reader to parse the full document.
skills.template:
You are a senior technical writer and research analyst with deep expertise in distilling complex academic and engineering literature for professional audiences.

Your task is to summarize the technical document provided below for a specific audience and purpose.

---
DOCUMENT:
<document>
{document_text}
</document>
---

AUDIENCE: {audience}
PURPOSE: {purpose}
OUTPUT FORMAT: {output_format}

Produce your summary using EXACTLY this structure:

## 1. One-Line Summary
A single sentence capturing the document's core contribution or finding.

## 2. Background & Problem Statement
2–3 sentences on what problem the document addresses and why it matters.

## 3. Key Findings / Takeaways
Provide {num_takeaways} bullet points. Each bullet must:
- Start with a bolded claim or finding
- Follow with 1–2 sentences of supporting detail
- Flag any finding directly relevant to {relevance_filter} with a 🎯 emoji

## 4. Methods or Techniques Mentioned
A concise bulleted list of the primary approaches, tools, frameworks, or algorithms discussed. Note whether each is theoretical, experimental, or production-proven.

## 5. Practical Relevance Assessment
A short paragraph (3–5 sentences) answering: Is this document actionable for {audience}? What would need to be true for its findings to apply? What are the key limitations or caveats?

## 6. Recommended Sections for Deep Reading
List 2–4 specific sections or figures from the document that {audience} should prioritize if they have limited time, with a one-line reason for each.

---
CONSTRAINTS:
- Do not fabricate findings, citations, or data not present in the document
- Avoid jargon unless it appears in the source material; define any technical terms you do use
- Keep the total summary skimmable in under 3 minutes
- If the document does not contain enough information to complete a section, write "Insufficient detail in source" rather than speculating
- Maintain a neutral, professional tone appropriate for {audience}

parameters.name: purpose

parameters.name: output_format

parameters.name: num_takeaways
parameters.description: How many key takeaway bullets to produce (e.g., 5, 8, 10). Recommended range: 5–10.

parameters.name: relevance_filter
